elements.sent-2411-001: In the past decades, research on the doubly heavy baryons developed rapidly in both experiments and theories.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work we study the mass spectra and semileptonic decay widths of the doubly heavy $\Xi$ and $\Omega$ baryons in the framework of the hypercentral constituent quark model.
elements.sent-2411-003: We solve the six-dimensional Schrödinger equation via deep learning and particle swarm optimization techniques to improve the speed and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then, we predict the masses of the ground and excited states of single and doubly heavy baryons.
elements.sent-2411-005: Working close to the zero recoil point, we also study the $ b \rightarrow c $ semileptonic decay widths and branching ratios of doubly heavy $\Xi$ and $\Omega$ baryons.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our results are compared to the exist experimental data and other theoretical evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our predictions of mass spectrum and decay widths provide valuable information for the experiment searching for undiscovered heavy baryon states.
elements.sent-2411-008: The spectroscopy of doubly heavy baryons has become one of the hot topics in hadronic physics.
elements.sent-2411-009: During the last few years significant progress has been made in heavy baryon physics and many theoretical progresses have been achieved in doubly and triply heavy baryon spectroscopy~\cite{Weng2018,Shaha2017,Garcilazo2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: The mass spectrum and width are two determinative quantities for theoretical and experimental researches and theoretical studies for the mass spectra and decay widths of heavy baryons are of special interest.
elements.sent-2411-011: The first observation of doubly heavy baryons was reported by the SELEX Collaboration for doubly heavy $M_{\Xi ^{++ _{cc }$ baryon.}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Later, the LHCb Collaboration reported the new baryon mass for the $ \Xi ^{++ _{cc}$ state as $ 3621.40\pm0.72\pm0.14\pm0.27$ MeV and $ 3620.6\pm1.5\pm0.4\pm0.3 $ MeV obtained in different decay modes\cite{LHCb2017,LHCb2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Recently, the LHCb collaboration has carried out new searches for the $\Xi_{bc}$ and $\Omega_{bc}$ doubly heavy baryons with a data sample of proton-proton collisions, but these baryon states are not yet to be observed~\cite{LHCb2020,LHCb2021,LHCb2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Although the $\Xi_{bc}$, $\Omega_{bc}$, $\Xi_{bb}$ and $\Omega_{bb}$ states has not been yet found in experiments, they are expected to be observed in the near future ~\cite{Ali2018,Ali PLB2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: The production of doubly heavy baryons can be done at the LHC or future high luminosity $ e^+e^- $ colliers.
elements.sent-2411-017: For the singly heavy baryons, mass spectra and some semileptonic decays have been measured in experiments~\cite{Navas2024,Aaij2017,Yelton2018,Aubert2008,Solovieva2009,Ablikim2017,Ablikim2018,Li2021a,Li2021b,Ammar2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In comparison to the doubly charmed $\Xi_{cc $ baryon, searching for the other doubly heavy baryon states are more complicated.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The search for these states is really hard for the experiment since higher energy and higher beam luminosity are required to produce them.
elements.sent-2411-020: For the semileptonic decay widths of doubly heavy baryons no experimental data are also reported and only a limited number of theoretical calculations are available.
elements.sent-2411-021: As there are more doubly heavy baryons that may be obtained by experiment, proposing a theoretical model for their structures is essential ~\cite{Li2023,Shi2020,Gutsche2019,Yu2019,Hernandez2008,Lyubovitskij2003,Albertus2005,Albertus2005, Albertus2008,Wang2017,Qin2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In recent years, machine learning has been the most popular computational approach for different fields of modern science and a significant raise of interest has been seen in that area.
elements.sent-2411-023: The machine learning techniques enable us to improve the performance and accuracy in theory and experiment.
elements.sent-2411-024: The solution of three-body Schrödinger equation is complicated in hadron physics.
elements.sent-2411-025: The deep neural network (DNN) as a subfield of machine learning techniques provides a solution to this problem.
elements.sent-2411-026: Deep learning algorithms can identify the energy eigenvalues of the baryon systems.
elements.sent-2411-027: Use of neural networks for solving differential equations has some advantages \cite{Yadav:2015,Parisi:2003 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: One point is that by increasing the dimensions of problem, the computational complexity does not increase considerably.
elements.sent-2411-029: The other advantage is that the solution of the problem is continuous over the domain of integration \cite{Mutuk: EPJA2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: In this study, we present a mathematical model using the DNN combining with particle swarm optimization algorithm (PSO) which is fast and provides high accuracy, to estimate the masses of the single and doubly heavy baryons.
elements.sent-2411-031: Some earlier works studied the heavy hadron properties via neural networks and deep learning techniques \cite{Mutuk: EPJA2020,Gal2022,Malekhosseini:PRD2024,Mutuk:CPC2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Ref. \cite{Mutuk: EPJA2020 solved the six-dimensional nonrelativistic Schrödinger equation via an artificial neural network and obtained the mass spectra of heavy $\Omega_b$ baryon states in a hypercentral approach.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Ref. \cite{Gal2022 used the neural networks and Gaussian processes to calculate the masses of the light baryons, charmonium and pentaquark states.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The authors of Ref. \cite{Malekhosseini:PRD2024 presented two approaches using the deep neural networks to predict the mass and width of the ordinary and exotic mesons.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Ref. \cite{Mutuk:CPC2019 solved five-body Schrödinger equation of hidden-charm pentaquark resonances by using the artificial neural network method and made predictions of parities for these states.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In this work, we study the single and doubly heavy baryon states in a hypercentral constituent quark model.
elements.sent-2411-037: Firstly, we introduce our model and simplify the three-body Schrödinger equation using the hypercentral model.
elements.sent-2411-038: Then, by applying the deep learning techniques, we evaluate the energy eigenvalues of the considered baryon systems.
elements.sent-2411-039: In our method, we also use the particle swarm optimization to get the best values of the energies.
elements.sent-2411-040: In the next step, we calculate the mass spectra of the ground and orbitally excited states of the single and doubly heavy baryons and compare our results to the exist experimental data and other predictions.
elements.sent-2411-041: The exist experimental data of the single heavy baryon masses help us to optimize our mass evaluations of the $ \Xi $ and $ \Omega $ doubly heavy baryons.
elements.sent-2411-042: Working close to the zero recoil point, we simplify the form factors of $ b\rightarrow c $ semileptonic decays and evaluate the semileptonic decay widths and branching ratios of the doubly heavy $ \Xi $ and $ \Omega $ baryons.
elements.sent-2411-043: It helps us to get a deep understanding of their structure.

elements.sent-2411-053: In order to obtain the heavy baryon masses we need to calculate the energy eigenvalues and also the eigenfunctions of the baryon states.
elements.sent-2411-054: We study the heavy baryons and therefore, the non-relativistic formalism is an appropriate method.
elements.sent-2411-055: We consider the baryon system as a bound state of three constituent quarks.
elements.sent-2411-056: Using the hypercentral constituent quark model \cite{Giannini:1998,Giannini:2 we can simplify the three-body problem of the baryon states.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The configurations of three quarks can be described by the Jacoobi coordinates, $ \rho $ and $ \lambda $, \begin{equation}\label{rhoo} \vec{\rho} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vec{r_1} - \vec{r_2}), \qquad \vec{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(\vec{r_1} + \vec{r_2} - 2 \vec{r_3})\end{equation} such that \begin{equation}m_{\rho} = \frac{2 m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}, \qquad m_{\lambda} = \frac{2 m_3 (m_1^2 + m_2^2+m_1m_2)}{(m_1+m_2) (m_1+ m_2 + m_3)}\end{equation} Here $m_1$, $m_2$ and $m_3$ are the constituent quark masses.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}m_{\rho} = \frac{2 m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}, \qquad m_{\lambda} = \frac{2 m_3 (m_1^2 + m_2^2+m_1m_2)}{(m_1+m_2) (m_1+ m_2 + m_3)}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-058: We introduce the hyperspherical coordinates, hyperradius $x$ and hyperangle $\zeta$ defined in terms of the Jacoobi coordinates $\rho$ and $\lambda$ by \begin{equation}x = \sqrt{\rho^2 + \lambda^2}, \qquad \xi = \arctan (\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\lambda}}).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-059: The Hamiltonian of three-body baron system takes the form \begin{equation}H=\frac{p^{2}_{\rho}}{2m_{\rho}}+\frac{p^{2}_{\lambda}}{2m_{\lambda}}+V(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-060: The wave function of a three-body system can be defined in terms of the hyperspherical harmonic basis: \begin{equation}\label{Psi} \Psi(\rho,\lambda)=\Sigma_{\gamma,l_{\rho},l_{\lambda}} N_{\gamma}\psi_{\nu,\gamma}(x)Y_{[\gamma],l_{\rho},l_{\lambda}}(\Omega_{\rho},\Omega_{\lambda},\xi).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-062: We consider the Killingbeck potential given by \cite{Ghalenovi:2014 \begin{equation}\label{v(x)} V(x)=\alpha x^2+\beta x-\frac{\tau}{x},\end{equation} here, $x$ is the hyperradius and $\alpha $, $\beta$ and $\tau$ are constant.}
elements.sent-2411-063: In the present work, we solve the Schrödinger equation \ref{Schro 2 using deep learning technique combined with a PSO algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-064: We use a trial wave function as supposed in our previous works~\cite{Ghalenovi:2022dok,Ghalenovi:2018fxh .}
elements.sent-2411-065: In our study we consider the baryon states with zero nodes $\nu=0 $, and neglect the spin dependent interactions.
elements.section-2411-003: Deep Learning Model for Energy Evaluation
elements.sent-2411-066: Neural network is a computational model motivated by the biological nervous system and is made up of computing units, called neurons.
elements.sent-2411-067: The first step in our framework involves the generation of a synthetic dataset by solving the time-independent Schrödinger equation and using the shooting method.
elements.sent-2411-068: The Schrödinger equation for the system under study is expressed as \begin{equation}\left[ \frac{d^2}{dx^2} + \frac{5}{x} \frac{d}{dx} - \frac{\gamma(\gamma + 4)}{x^2} - 2ma x^2 - 2mbx + \frac{2mc}{x} \right] \psi_\gamma(x) = -2m E_\gamma \psi_\gamma(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-069: The goal is to compute $E_{\gamma}$ energy eigenvalues such that the boundary conditions for $ \psi_\gamma(x) $ are satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-070: Here deep learning model is designed to predict energy eigenvalues based on the features in dataset.
elements.sent-2411-071: The deep neural network architecture consists of three layers: an input layer, hidden layers, and output layer.
elements.sent-2411-072: These layers are connected to the next layer, however there is no connection among the neurons in the same layer.
elements.sent-2411-073: They are ideal tools to solve differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-074: In this study, we consider a feedforward neural network with one input layer, two hidden layers and one output layer.
elements.sent-2411-075: The output layer consists of a single neuron to predict the $ E_\gamma $ energy eigenvalues.
elements.sent-2411-076: The neural network is trained using the mean squared error (MSE) loss function: \begin{equation}MSE = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_{\text{true}} - Y_{\text{pred}})^2,\end{equation} where $ n$, $ Y_{\text{true $ and $ Y_{\text{pred $ are the number of data points, actual value for $ i $-th data point and predicted value for the $ i $-th data point.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: We employ the adaptive moment estimation optimizer for adaptive learning rate adjustments during training.
elements.sent-2411-078: The model is trained over a large number of epochs to ensure convergence.
elements.sent-2411-079: The dataset is split into 80\% training and 20\% validation subsets.
elements.sent-2411-080: The performance of the model can be evaluated by using the root mean squared error (RMSE) and MSE on the validation set to the prediction accuracy assessment.
elements.sent-2411-081: For the further refines of energy evaluations, we employ particle swarm optimization.
elements.sent-2411-082: PSO is a population-based optimization algorithm inspired by the social behavior of birds, and is effective in finding global optima in multi-dimensional search spaces.
elements.sent-2411-083: A swarm of particles is initialized randomly, where each particle represents a solution (energy value).
elements.sent-2411-084: The position of each particle is updated iteratively, and the fitness of each particle is evaluated using the trained deep learning model.
elements.sent-2411-085: The fitness function for each particle is based on the difference between the predicted energy value and the target energy value.
elements.sent-2411-086: The goal is to minimize this difference.
elements.sent-2411-087: The global best solution found by PSO algorithm represents the optimal energy estimations.
elements.sent-2411-088: By using the obtained $E_{\gamma}$ energies, we are able to evaluate the mass spectra of the baryon systems.
elements.sent-2411-089: The quark masses used in the present work are taken from our previous work~\cite{Ghalenovi:2022dok .}
elements.sent-2411-090: We consider $m_{q}$=320 MeV, $m_{s}$=440 MeV, $m_{c}$=1600 MeV and $m_{b}$=4670 MeV.

elements.sent-2411-129: In the present work, we propose our phenomenological approach for estimation energy eigenvalues of three-body baryon systems by integrating the deep learning with particle swarm optimization methods.
elements.sent-2411-130: By integrating deep learning with PSO, we have developed a powerful methodology for accurate energy prediction in three-body quantum systems.
elements.sent-2411-131: The deep learning model, trained on a large dataset generated using the shooting method, provides an initial estimate of the energy values while, PSO refines these predictions and leads to highly accuracy in calculations.
elements.sent-2411-132: This hybrid approach demonstrates the potential of combining machine learning and optimization techniques in solving challenging math problems in computational physics.

elements.sent-2411-166: In the heavy-quark sector of heavy baryons one can use a non-relativistic formalism to study these states.
elements.sent-2411-167: The configurations of three quarks of baryons can be described by the Jacoobi coordinates, $ \rho $ and $ \lambda $, \begin{equation}\label{rhoo} \vec{\rho} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}(\vec{r_1} - \vec{r_2}), \qquad \vec{\lambda} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}}(\vec{r_1} + \vec{r_2} - 2 \vec{r_3})\end{equation} such that \begin{equation}m_{\rho} = \frac{2 m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}, \qquad m_{\lambda} = \frac{2 m_3 (m_1^2 + m_2^2+m_1m_2)}{(m_1+m_2) (m_1+ m_2 + m_3)}\end{equation} Here $m_1$, $m_2$ and $m_3$ are the constituent quark masses.
elements.sent-2411-168: In the hypercentral model, the hyperradius $x$ and hyperangle $\zeta$ are given in terms of the Jacoobi coordinates $\rho$ and $\lambda$ \cite{Giannini:1998,Giannini:2 by \begin{equation}x = \sqrt{\rho^2 + \lambda^2}, \qquad \xi = \arctan (\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\lambda}}).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-169: The Hamiltonian of the baron system takes the form \begin{equation}H=\frac{p^{2}_{\rho}}{2m_{\rho}}+\frac{p^{2}_{\lambda}}{2m_{\lambda}}+V(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-171: In the present work, we solve the Schrödinger equation \ref{Schro using deep learning technique combined with a PSO algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-172: We use a trial wave function as proposed in our previous works~\cite{Ghalenovi:2022dok,Ghalenovi:2018fxh .}
elements.sent-2411-173: In the present study we consider the baryon states with zero nodes $\nu=0 $, and neglect the spin dependent interactions.
elements.sent-2411-174: The first step in our framework involves the generation of a synthetic dataset by solving the Schrödinger equation and using the shooting method.
elements.sent-2411-175: The Schrödinger equation for the system under study is expressed as \begin{equation}\label{Schro2} \left[ \frac{d^2}{dx^2} + \frac{5}{x} \frac{d}{dx} - \frac{\gamma(\gamma + 4)}{x^2} - 2m\alpha x^2 - 2m\beta x + \frac{2m\tau}{x} \right] \psi_\gamma(x) = -2m E_\gamma \psi_\gamma(x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-176: In this step deep learning model is used to predict energy eigenvalues based on the features in dataset.
elements.sent-2411-177: The deep neural networks consist of three layers: an input layer, hidden layers, and output layer.
elements.sent-2411-178: In the present work, we employ a feed forward neural network with one input layer, two hidden layers and one output layer to solve the differential equation \ref{Schro2 .}
elements.sent-2411-179: The root mean squared error (RMSE) and MSE show the validity of our model and its accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-180: For further refines of energy evaluations, we employ the particle swarm optimization.
elements.sent-2411-181: PSO is an effective method in finding global optima in multi-dimensional search spaces.
